english: ground, but in Tibet it signifies a place to which corpses are brought to be cut into pieces for hungry dogs and vultures, this being considered a very honourable mode of burying (or rather disposing of) dead bodies, Köpp. II, 322. These places of course are haunted by demons and foul spirits; dúr-krod-pa (dur khrod pa) an ascetic living at such a place, Burn I, 309. — dúr-rgyas (dur rgyas) the last food which a dying man eats. — dúr-sgam (dur sgam), dúr-sgrom (dur sgrom) coffin. — dúr-rdo (dur rdo) tomb-stone Cs. — dúr-spyaṅ (dur spyang) jackal. — dúr-pʻuṅ (dur phung) barrow, tumulus, mound, cairn. — dúr-byaṅ (dur byang) epitaph Cs. — dúr-tsun (dur tsun), dúr-tsod (dur tsod), food offered to the dead Cs. — dur-mtséd (dur mtshed) a place for burning dead bodies Sch. — dúr-sri (dur sri) a grave-devil, a sort of sepulchral vampire.

1. sbst. weed, weeds, Sch. — 2. vb. to run Mil., dúr-te (dur te) rgyúg-pa (rgyug pa) to run towards a place or object, to hasten to, zás-la (zas la) dúr-ba (dur ba) to hasten to dinner, lás-la (las la) to work C.; cf. ṅám-dur-ċan (ngam dur can).
a purgative root, prob. = tạ́r-nu (thar nu) S.g., acc. to Wdk. = tri-byi-ta (tri byi ta) (sic), prop. त्रिवृता, Ipomoea Turpethum.

prop. pf. of ºdúl-ba ('dul ba), soft, of the skin etc.; tame; gentle (temper), easy (disposition), mild; also sbst. softness etc.; dul-po (dul po), W. *dúl-mo* (*dul mo*) id., but only adj.; ma dul-ba (ma dul ba) untamed, rude, Dzl.; *sráb-kʻa (*srab kha) (or kʻá-po (kha po)) dúl-mo* (*dul mo*) W. soft- or tender-mouthed; tame, manageable, tractable. Tar. 11, 14 a better reading prob. would be: dbaṅ-po (dbang po) ºdul-bai ('dul ba'i) brjìd (brjid) a splendour that dazzles the senses.
a kind of water-colour made of pulverized gold and silver, for painting and writing.

1. time, in general, dús-kyi (dus kyi) kór-lo ('khor lo) v. kór-lo ('khor lo); dús-kyi (dus kyi) means also: happening sometimes Mil.; dus (dus) adv., for a while, for some time Lt.; deï (de'i) dus-su (dus su), dus (dus) de tsa-na (de tsa na), dé-dus (de dus), dus der (dus der), at the time, at this time; dus de-ñyíd-du (dus de nyid du) then immediately, directly afterwards; dáṅ-poi (dang po'i) dus ñyíd-du (dus nyid du) in the very first time; dús-su (dus su), or dus-dús-su (dus dus su), dus ºga-ré (dus 'ga' re), sometimes, now and then; de daṅ (de dang) dus mñyám-du (dus mnyam du) simultaneously with that Glr.; dus yćig-tu (dus gcig tu) or la (la) at one and the same time, together; dús-ċig-na (dus cig na) (erron. yćig (gcig)), also dus re (dus re) (or nam (nam))-žig-gi (zhig gi) tse (tshe), dus-re (dus re) (-žig (-zhig)), once, one day, some day; dus lan-ċig (dus lan cig) id. Glr.; dus pʻyi žíg-na (dus phyi zhig na) some future day; dus yžan žig-na (dus gzhan zhig na) another time; dus ċi tsam-na (dus ci tsam na) at what time? when? Glr.; dus(-na) (dus (-na)) after a genit., inf., or verbal root = when, after, žag yñyis soṅ (zhag gnyis song) dus (dus) when two days had, or will have passed Mil.; ṅa (nga) bú-moi (bu mo'i) dús-na (dus na) yin-te (yin te) when I was still a girl Glr.; mgú-dus (mgu dus) med (med) the time of being satisfied never arrives Mil.; btsá-dus-te (btsa' dus te) as the time of giving birth has come Lt.; frq. with ºbáb-pa ('bab pa): bdag (bdag) ºdúl-bai ('dul ba'i) dús-la (dus la) bab (bab) the time of my conversion has come; sometimes dús-la (dus la) sleb (sleb) Lt.; col.: dus sleb (dus sleb) the time is come; ºgro-bai ('gro ba'i) dus ºdébs-pa ('debs pa) Dzl., byéd-pa (byed pa) frq., to fix a time for going, also thus: nam ºgró-bai (nam 'gro ba'i) dus byéd-pa (dus byed pa) Dzl.; dus kún-tu (dus kun tu), dus rgyún-du (dus rgyun du) always; almost pleon. in: dus dá-nas (dus da nas) henceforth, from this time forward Mil.; de daṅ (de dang) dus ºdzom (dus 'dzom) as to time it coincides with that Glr. — 2. the right time, proper season; for is expressed by the genit. of the inf. (cf. above: the time of my conversion); dús-su (dus su) at the right or proper time, e.g. for paying off Glr.; dus ma yin-pa (dus ma yin pa) the wrong time; dus ma yin-par (dus ma yin par), dus-mín (dus min) unseasonably, not in due time; esp. too soon, prematurely, e.g. to die; dus-ma-yin-pa spóṅ-ba (dus ma yin pa spong ba) to abstain from doing unseasonable things. — 3 dus ysum (dus gsum) the three times, viz. dú-ltai (da lta'i), or dá-ltar-gyi (da ltar gyi), ºdás-pai ('das pa'i), and ma-ºóṅs-pai (ma 'ongs pa'i), frq., thus in dus ysúm-gyi (dus gsum gyi) saṅs-rgyás (sangs rgyas) the Buddhas of the three times; often also with special reference to metempsychosis, the present, the former, and the future period of life; with respect to the times of the day: morning, noon, evening; besides ñyín-dus (nyin dus) ysum (gsum), also mtsán-dus (mtshan dus) ysum (gsum) occurs. —
